result:
There is something profoundly humbling about standing at the edge of the ocean at midnight. In the absence of the sun, the sea transforms from a sprawling blue canvas into an endless, churning void. The horizon vanishes entirely, leaving only the sound of the water rushing against the sand—a rhythmic, ancient heartbeat that has measured the earth’s time long before we arrived to listen. It is a darkness that swallows shapes and silhouettes, turning the world into an experience built entirely on sound and touch. 

When you stand there, shivering in the salt-spray, the anxieties of the day shrink into insignificance. The ocean does not care about your emails, your deadlines, or your social obligations. It is indifferent, vast, and wonderfully impersonal. Yet, rather than feeling alienated by this indifference, I find it deeply comforting. We spend so much of our lives trying to be seen, trying to leave a mark, but the midnight tide reminds us that we are fleeting visitors on a rock spinning through the dark. 

There is a quiet freedom in that impermanence. We are granted these brief, flickering moments to witness the sublime, to feel the cold wind on our faces, and to listen to the water pull the shore back into the deep. And when morning comes, the light will return, the horizon will reappear, and the world will resume its loud, demanding pace. But for a few quiet hours, under a ceiling of distant stars, there is only the tide, the dark, and the peace of being impossibly small.
